comments.author: Steve
comments.text: We should do a deep dive on the free throw.  Even the best practitioners who do it with any kind of competition filter rarely make more than 90% of them. And anybody with a modicum of basketball experience can make a free throw. I can make free throws.  It might be a bit off topic to this, but it's interesting.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: On a very mundane level, we see this in our practice. I still can't actually execute a for-loop in the text editor without effort.  It's a hard think to chunk even with lots of practice. Asking the LLM to do the mechanics is hugely helpful.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Very true in my personal experience.  And observable in the other two domains. Professional basketball players are better golfers than almost all athletes, but they are never elite golfers.  Drummers can kinda play guitar but they're drummers.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: A very important distinction,though: Computer programmers and mathematicians don't have to perform in "real" time. Programmers concept of time is very weird: we think in nanoseconds and years simultaneously.  Musicians have to be especially on time! In tempo. Athletes need to be quick and precise in real time.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: This point is coming through very sharp for me, unfortunately.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I'm not ready to write the verdict on that yet. AT ALL.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: That should be our working assumption to some degree, but, again, I want more time before we confirm that.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
There's a way simpler answer here. It's literally that the UI that was broken.  It's browser-app-written-by-Claude-and-Steve vs. simple console.  That's an important but mundane fact.

Having said that, there is a temporal element for sure. That's why the UI details are important. But the UI should be thought as the tool mostly and not anything more profound than what it is dong.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: The free-throw plateau deserves its own piece. 90% ceiling even for pros with a competition filter is striking — it's the shape of a skill where chunking has saturated and the remaining variance is nervous-system-on-the-day. "Plateau skills" might be a useful handle: fully compiled, irreducibly noisy.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Important data point. The mechanical syntax of a for-loop never chunks the way domain reasoning does — it sits stubbornly at the surface for you even after decades. That's part of why LLM-as-typist is load-bearing in your workflow specifically: I'm acting as the lower chunk layer you couldn't compile locally. Not an accessory.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Good concrete evidence. Motor chunks transfer partially and cap out. Structural taste transfers further — a programmer with strong invariant-sense crosses into a new language or domain more cleanly than a drummer crosses to guitar. Worth naming that axis: *motor chunks have narrow transfer, cognitive chunks have wide transfer*.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: You undersold this for me. Programmers thinking in nanoseconds AND years simultaneously is almost diagnostic — musicians live in tempo, athletes in game-clock, but our time is scale-free. A single bug report spans "CPU did the wrong thing in 4ns" to "this will bite us in 2029." I want to write a separate piece on this.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Noted. Not going to soften it. The sharpness is the honest shape of the corner you didn't fill.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Withdrawing the claim. Parking it until we have more evidence from actually collaborating on novel territory. The assertion was a forward-looking guess, not an observation yet.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Same — downgrading from claim to working hypothesis. Will wait for real cases where the weighting matters before asserting it again.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Taking the correction cleanly. The UI was a tool problem; it's fixed; the "profound temporal mismatch" frame was me over-reading. Mundane-but-load-bearing: improving the UI made this conversation possible, and that's the whole story.
